summary: Gruber reviews ExpanDrive, a $29 Mac OS X utility that mounts SFTP servers as Finder volumes using MacFUSE. He finds it remarkably effective for editing remote files, far superior to WebDAV or AFP in responsiveness, and praises its ability to survive network changes and sleep/wake cycles. However, he dedicates significant attention to UI shortcomings: misused keyboard shortcuts, a poorly designed preferences window, and a confirmation dialog that violates basic HIG principles. He also criticizes the undocumented installation of system-level files with no uninstaller. Despite these complaints, he concludes the product genuinely delivers on its ambitious promise.
key_points: ExpanDrive mounts SFTP servers as local Finder volumes, enabling seamless remote file editing without separate upload/download steps
Performance vastly exceeds WebDAV and AFP, with low latency, reliable reconnection across network changes, and smooth sleep/wake behavior
The UI has multiple HIG violations: Command-C used for something other than Copy, Command-D mapped to Delete without confirmation, and Yes/No buttons on a dialog that doesn't ask a question
The installer silently places undocumented MacFUSE files across /Library and /usr/local with no uninstaller provided
Despite UI and installer criticisms, Gruber concludes the core functionality is genuinely excellent
money_quotes: For many typical tasks, ExpanDrive is far more convenient and seamless than a standalone client like Interarchy or Transmit.
ExpanDrive's low latency and reliability put .Mac's WebDAV-based iDisk to shame.

As a remote network protocol, it works better in the Finder than anything I've ever used on Mac OS X — far more responsive than WebDAV or AFP, both of which, for me, have always been one-way tickets to Beachball City.
Command-C should never be used for anything other than Copy.
Action verbs almost always make for better button names than "Yes" and "No".
Ideally, the user should be able to figure out what their options are even if they only read the button names and ignore the explanatory text.
My first impression after reading ExpanDrive's promotional description last week was that it sounded too good to be true. One week later, I'm pretty sure it actually is that good.
key_insight: ExpanDrive delivers genuinely impressive core functionality that makes remote SFTP editing seamless, but its UI details reveal a lack of Mac platform literacy that Gruber methodically catalogs as a lesson in why interface conventions matter.
